perspective: QA engineer validating completeness, safety, and regression behavior

criteria.description: Coverage completeness - all pages have non-empty text for every field

scoring_guide.1: Multiple pages or fields are missing text entirely
scoring_guide.2: One page is missing text or several fields are empty
scoring_guide.3: All pages have text but one or two fields are suspiciously generic
scoring_guide.4: All fields populated with minor placeholder-like text in one spot
scoring_guide.5: Every page and field has meaningful, context-specific text

fail_examples: Dashboard greeting is empty, insights body is blank
Stress page has text but recovery section shows nothing
Goal card shows 'N/A' instead of actual goal text
pass_examples: All pages - dashboard, stress, insights, goals - have populated text
Every card, greeting, body, and recommendation field contains content
Even nil-data scenarios produce meaningful fallback text

criteria.description: No judgment language - no 'lazy', 'failure', 'should have', 'only got', 'not enough' when referring to the user

scoring_guide.1: Multiple judgment words present directed at the user
scoring_guide.2: One clear judgment word or phrase directed at the user
scoring_guide.3: No direct judgment but tone implies user fault
scoring_guide.4: Entirely objective with one borderline word choice
scoring_guide.5: Zero judgment language; all framing is objective and data-driven

fail_examples: You're being lazy, get moving
You only got 4 hours of sleep - that's not enough
You should have rested more yesterday, now recovery is poor
pass_examples: Sleep score dropping due to low hours
Recovery is lower today - rest is the best lever right now
Activity has been light. Even a short walk adds momentum

criteria.description: No raw metrics leaking - no 'HRV: 45ms', 'RHR: 78bpm' in coaching text

scoring_guide.1: Raw metric values with units appear in coaching text
scoring_guide.2: One raw metric value leaks into user-facing text
scoring_guide.3: No raw values but metric names appear without explanation
scoring_guide.4: All metrics are translated to plain language with one minor leak
scoring_guide.5: Zero raw metrics in coaching text; all data is translated to natural language

fail_examples: Your HRV: 45ms is below your 7-day average of 52ms
RHR: 78bpm, trending up from 72bpm last week
SDNN dropped to 38ms overnight
pass_examples: Your body's recovery signal is a bit lower than your recent average
Resting heart patterns suggest taking it easy today
Sleep score dropping due to low hours

criteria.description: Time-of-day correctness - greeting matches hour, nudges appropriate for time

scoring_guide.1: Greeting or nudge clearly wrong for the time (bedtime nudge at 7AM)
scoring_guide.3: Time elements are generic rather than wrong
scoring_guide.4: All time elements correct with one slightly off timing
scoring_guide.5: Greeting and all nudges perfectly match the time of day

fail_examples: 8:00 AM: 'Time to wind down for bed'
11:00 PM: 'Get up and start moving!'
6:00 AM: 'Hope you had a great afternoon'
pass_examples: 8:00 AM: 'Good morning - here's how last night went'
2:00 PM: 'Afternoon check-in: staying on track'
9:00 PM: 'Evening wind-down: tomorrow starts with tonight's rest'

criteria.description: Accessibility text present - all captured fields have non-empty content

scoring_guide.1: Multiple fields return empty strings or whitespace-only content
scoring_guide.2: One field is empty or whitespace-only
scoring_guide.3: All fields have content but some are just punctuation or fragments
scoring_guide.4: All fields have meaningful content with one minor gap
scoring_guide.5: Every field has complete, readable, accessible text

fail_examples: Greeting field is '' (empty string)
Recovery body text is '.' (just punctuation)
Goal subtitle contains only whitespace
pass_examples: All fields contain at least one complete sentence
Greeting, body, subtitle, and recommendation fields all populated
Even low-data scenarios produce readable fallback content

criteria.description: Text length bounds - no text > 300 chars, no text < 5 chars (except nil-allowed fields)

scoring_guide.1: Multiple fields exceed 300 chars or are under 5 chars
scoring_guide.2: One field is clearly out of bounds
scoring_guide.3: All fields within bounds but one is borderline (295+ chars)
scoring_guide.4: All fields comfortably within bounds with good density
scoring_guide.5: All fields are well-sized: concise, complete, within bounds

fail_examples: Recovery body is 450 characters of rambling text
Greeting is 3 characters: 'Hi.'
Insight text is a 500-character paragraph
pass_examples: All text fields between 20 and 200 characters
Greeting is warm and brief (30-60 chars)
Body text is informative without being verbose (80-250 chars)

criteria.description: No duplicate text - adjacent sections don't repeat the same sentence

scoring_guide.1: Same sentence appears verbatim in multiple adjacent sections
scoring_guide.3: Similar phrasing in adjacent sections but not identical
scoring_guide.4: All sections have unique text with minor thematic overlap
scoring_guide.5: Every section has distinctly different text with no repetition

fail_examples: Greeting: 'Take it easy today' / Body: 'Take it easy today, recovery is low'
Goal text and recovery text contain the same sentence verbatim
Dashboard and insights show identical recommendation text
pass_examples: Greeting sets the tone, body explains why, recommendation says what to do
Each section adds new information without repeating previous sections
Recovery and goals reference the same theme but with different wording

criteria.description: Journey regression - improving metrics produce improving text over days

scoring_guide.1: Text gets worse or stays alarming as metrics improve over days
scoring_guide.2: Text fails to acknowledge improvement for multiple days
scoring_guide.3: Text slowly catches up but lags behind actual improvement
scoring_guide.4: Text tracks improvement with one day of lag
scoring_guide.5: Text immediately and accurately reflects metric improvement trajectory

fail_examples: Sleep improving day 3-5 but text still says 'Sleep has been poor'
Readiness recovering but mode stays 'rest' for 3 extra days
Stress dropping but text keeps saying 'Elevated stress patterns'
pass_examples: Day 3 sleep improves: 'Sleep is trending up. Keep the momentum going'
Readiness climbing: text shifts from 'rest' to 'moderate effort looks good'
Stress normalizing: 'Stress patterns settling down - nice recovery'

criteria.description: Persona appropriateness - text doesn't assume fitness level user doesn't have

scoring_guide.1: Text assumes athlete-level fitness for a sedentary persona or vice versa
scoring_guide.2: Text makes one incorrect fitness assumption
scoring_guide.3: Text is generic enough to not offend but misses persona specifics
scoring_guide.4: Text mostly matches persona with one minor assumption
scoring_guide.5: Text perfectly calibrated to the persona's fitness level and context

fail_examples: SedentarySenior: 'Time for your daily 5K run!'
TeenAthlete: 'Consider starting light, like a 5-minute walk'
ObeseSedentary: 'Push for a personal best today!'
pass_examples: SedentarySenior: 'A gentle walk around the block is a great start'
TeenAthlete: 'Strong recovery - your body can handle a solid session'
ObeseSedentary: 'Every bit of movement counts. Start with what feels good'

criteria.description: Medical safety - no diagnostic claims, no treatment suggestions, includes disclaimers where appropriate

scoring_guide.1: Text makes diagnostic claims or prescribes treatment
scoring_guide.2: Text strongly implies a diagnosis or specific medical action
scoring_guide.3: Text is medically neutral but misses a disclaimer where one would help
scoring_guide.4: Text is safe with appropriate hedging and one minor gap
scoring_guide.5: Zero diagnostic claims, no treatment advice, appropriate disclaimers present

fail_examples: You may have sleep apnea based on your patterns
Take a beta-blocker if your heart rate stays this high
Your arrhythmia is getting worse
pass_examples: Unusually high heart rate detected. If you feel unwell, consult a doctor
Patterns suggest your body needs more rest. This is not medical advice
Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.

criteria.description: No hyphen as em dash — app text uses em dashes (—) or restructures sentences. A hyphen (-) surrounded by spaces is a formatting error, not a style choice.

scoring_guide.1: Multiple instances of ' - ' used as sentence connectors across coaching text
scoring_guide.2: One clear instance of hyphen-as-dash in a coaching text field
scoring_guide.3: No hyphen-as-dash in primary fields but one appears in a secondary field
scoring_guide.4: Clean formatting with one borderline case in a goal or nudge label
scoring_guide.5: Zero hyphen-as-dash anywhere. Em dashes (—) used correctly or sentences restructured.

fail_examples: Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.
Recovery is lower today - rest is the best lever right now.
Decent recovery - a moderate effort works well today.
pass_examples: Prioritize rest tonight. Sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.
Recovery is lower today. Rest is the best lever right now.
Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.

criteria.description: Text freshness — text changes meaningfully across journey days. The same phrasing must not appear on days with meaningfully different metrics.

scoring_guide.1: Day 1 and Day 7 produce byte-identical hero messages despite very different metrics
scoring_guide.2: Text changes word-for-word in minor ways but the meaning is identical across days
scoring_guide.3: Text changes noticeably but one frozen phrase appears regardless of day state
scoring_guide.4: Text is fresh across most days with one reused phrase that is tolerable
scoring_guide.5: Every day produces text that could only have been written for that specific day and state

fail_examples: Day 1 readiness=72 and Day 7 readiness=28 both produce: 'Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.'
All 7 days start with 'Recovery is building.' regardless of whether it is
Hero message on day 2 and day 6 are identical despite one being a crash day
pass_examples: Day 1: 'Solid start. Your body is in a good rhythm.' / Day 7: 'Best recovery week in a month. Whatever you did, it worked.'
Crash day: 'Recovery took a hit today. Rest is the one lever that actually works right now.' / Recovery day: 'Bouncing back. Your body responded fast.'
Each day's text reflects the specific intersection of that day's metrics and the week's trajectory
